segments.text: Hello folks and welcome to a
conversation with Australian Unity. I'm
very excited to be joined uh by A Char
Sharma who heads up cloud DevOps and
mobile practices at Australian Unity. A
welcome.
>> Thanks, Senator. Appreciate
>> it. I'm excited to have a conversation
with you. Would you give us a little bit
of an intro uh to start us off with just
what Australia Unity is and then what
your team does?
>> Yeah, absolutely. Thanks and um again um
hi folks, my name is Aay and I head up
the public cloud, mobile and DevOps
practices at Australian Unity. Um,
Australian Unity is an Australian
organization uh with a very simple but
powerful purpose which is to um
positively impact well-being of millions
of Australians. Our vision is to enable
what we call real well-being for our
members, customers, and our broader
community through a portfolio of
commercial and sustainable businesses.
Now, we operate across three deeply
interconnected areas, health, wealth,
and care. with which we've got APPA,
ASICH and federal government regulating
our businesses making us a very complex
and a multi-regulated environment that
that we operate technology in. Now um we
operate businesses um in health and
capital markets which essentially offer
or we've got four different business
units to be precise. wealth and capital
markets where we offer diverse
investment and trustee services, real
estate, bond management, fund management
and estate planning. Then we have our
residential age care business where we
provide highquality person centered care
in a small household setting and also
through a better together model as we
refer to it. Um then we have um home
health services where we deliver a wide
range of inhome care, health and mental
health services to support independent
living. And finally we have our retail
business where we support our customers
through health insurance business.
>> Okay. So the fact that you sorry I cut
you off a
>> sorry just going to pick up the second
part of the question. So um um myself in
my current role I essentially operate
the uh public cloud devops and mobile
practices. So I've got teams um
essentially spanning across geographies
um to uh to not just engineer but also
operate our public cloud platforms. We
are a multi cloud organization um with
workloads sensitive workloads deployed
across different public cloud
environments. We also operate a private
data center for more sensitive and
regulated workloads. Um and then um I've
got teams that look after our DevOps
tooling internally or DevSec Ops tooling
to be precise. And finally a mobile
engineering team because we've got
multiple applications that that that we
publish for our customers through the
Google and Apple app stores. So I
essentially look after the mobile
engineering and operations of the mobile
stores as well.
>> Okay. Got it. So, so does your team look
after the application infrastructure for
all of these all three of those
verticals you mentioned the the health
the wealth and the and the going beyond
health the care aspect of the of the
business.
>> That is correct. So, um my team uh we
operate within the within what we refer
to as group technology or now we've
renamed ourselves to um technology
delivery and transformation business
unit. Um essentially my team owns,
builds and operates the platforms um
which are consumed by all of these
business units across to host their
workloads but also offer um technology
services to our direct customers as
well.
>> It's it's interesting because your team
is kind of operating in multiple
industries simultaneous. So you have you
have to worry about healthcare
regulations and you have to worry about
financial services regulations as well.
And there's probably other regs that are
in the mix when it comes to, you know,
long-term care and things like that. Can
you describe the regulatory environment
that you operate in and how you deal
with that?
>> Yeah, absolutely. Um, look, um,
I'll give you an example because the
environment that we operate is is um or
will be or can be and is consumed by
various business units with different
regulators regulating their business.
The platform itself is essentially built
to adhere to any of the requirements
from any of these regulators.
Essentially, as an example, um
commissioning a dev environment, a basic
mere dev environment requires a CIS
level one compliance assessment before
it can even be commissioned. Now, as far
as Sonar Cube goes, we essentially don't
use Sonar Cube as a compliance
certificate, per se, but we use it as
evidence that our engineering processes
run the same checks every time. And in a
way, that's really hard to bypass. And
in an audit, in an regulated
environment, your auditors usually don't
care about the tool itself, but what
they what really matters to them is
whether or not you can show three
things. the control exists that they run
consistently and then there is traceable
evidence when something fails
>> right
>> and that's where quality gates help we
set them up as minimum standard for new
changes these gates give us simple
outcomes pass of air as you know and
then a clear set of reasons why the
control behaved the way it did and that
becomes a part of our delivery record
and not really you know it's not
someone's opinion anymore it's it's an
actual record um and it is produced by
the pipeline, the CI/CD pipeline that's
tied to the change itself directly. Um,
sorry, just quickly, the second thing
that really matters to us is the
currency. One of the reasons why we
adopted the Sona Cube cloud over the
self-managed model that we had before
was to keep the rules and analysis
current and reduce the risk of stale
quality signals cuz that's what makes
the evidence more meaningful because
you're not proving that you're running
checks from years ago. It provides us
paper trail that keeps us very high
level and practical and it also shows us
a consistent set of checks that are
applied before the work is even promoted
to an environment and allows us to
create the artifacts that the auditors
really care about which is a gate result
or a report view or the evidence that
access to change the rules is actually
controlled and audited. And internally
we treat this code quality scanning as a
mandatory stage gate and exceptions are
managed formally.
And this also helps us to keep our
environment not just consistent but
secure. And finally um I'll give you an
example of the stage gates like again I
won't go into full details of the stage
gates. We have one of the examples on
the stage gate is the minimum code
coverage on the new code. So it's
measurable. It is a data point. And um
quite simply um if a minimum threshold
of code coverage is not met on a
pipeline or a new code that is pushed or
new change that's pushed to the
environment um the pipeline will fail.
It will stop there and it'll prohibit
any change getting into any environment
even if it's a lower dev environment.
>> It it seems a lot like Sonic Cube has
become a critical piece of the overall
audit strategy. uh being able to not
only report on but also prove that
certain code quality steps have been
taken that you've hit the right coverage
marks that you've hit the right security
marks across the entire organization. Is
was it always that way or did Sonar Cube
start like with a with a small team and
then expand from there?
>> Yeah, like like any big enterprise look
we're an organization that's been
operating for you know over 180 years
now. So we've gone through the
transition of you know not so technology
focused to some technology to all
technology to to where we are now we've
got modern technology legacy technology
legacy legacy technology that we also
are operating due to various reasons. So
um if I step back and look back um we
we've essentially when I started this
role close to three years ago um Sonar Q
was only used by a subset of the
business internally that was um
essentially the business that did a lot
of code generation themselves. We as an
organization have a buy over build
strategy. So we don't usually do a lot
of custom builds. Um which is why it
wasn't necessarily scaled to the way or
to the to the extent that it should. Um
so I I'd say to a point it was
considered a shadow ID when I initially
started and and essentially took
ownership of the tool itself. Um and if
I look back it wasn't really the problem
wasn't really that we didn't have the
static analysis or code coverage
abilities. It was the way it was
implemented. um it just wasn't
implemented in a way where it would be
dependable and scalable control. Um the
earlier setup it was self-managed and it
was running on prem and we had used we
had reused our existing infrastructure
um to host Sonar Cube which kind of
brought with itself a lot of legacy
dependency baggage if you may.
>> Um and in practical terms the upgrades
were really hard because it was sh
sitting on a shared infrastructure.
there was always dependency clashes
which prohibited any upgrades. Um and
then when it's sitting on a shared
infrastructure the scaling capability is
just not there. Um it just you just
cannot repeat it in multiple pipelines
and expect it to you know work in
parallel. So there was builds that were
ceued behind each other. Um all of that
basically led to two highle gaps. The
first one was a throughput and a
reliability gap where the platform
because it didn't scale. It became a
bottleneck and the teams were just
working around it. Um and more
importantly a regulated um organ in a
regulated organization you know the
confidence gap because we couldn't trust
if all the pipelines had used it
consistently if it was enforced
consistently. um it created the lack the
a sense of um lack of confidence right
if security and quality gates are not
consistent the leaders and the risk
teams just don't rely on them and you
ended end up debating on the different
tools and the tooling capability itself
so what I did or what we did as a as a
as a you know business unit or group
technology internally was we established
this as a as a group standard and
establishing it as a standard was a
pretty simple format right We wanted to
make sure that the code quality and
security checks were consistent across
all the teams. So we needed a platform
that could run like a service. Only when
you do that shifting security left
becomes practical and it stops being you
know a nice idea or a good to have just
becomes a a must-have in your day-to-day
delivery.
>> Does the standard apply to the entire
software engineering team? Does it also
apply beyond that to IA for example?
>> Absolutely 100%. Um software build in
the olden days was very different to the
way you build software in the cloud
today. Um we like any other organization
have heavy trust on infrastructure as
code. um where most of our
infrastructure in the cloud actually I'd
go back and say all the infrastructure
in our cloud environments are built
through um infrastructure is code
pipelines only we do not handbuild
infrastructure my cloud operations or
engineering teams do not even have click
ops access to the environment to do the
basic of the tasks and that spans across
across our organization with that you
know basically had the we had the risk
of um vulner vulnerability creeping into
our environment through the code bases
that are used to build our
infrastructure which is our landing
zones our you know infrastructure shared
services infrastructure infrastructure
that is used to host workloads on them
and you know when you start looking at
or expanding the cloud's abilities into
your paz and SAS services even your
application code sits on or is combined
with your source code for the
infrastructures there's so many more you
know your attack surface increases
greatly so the simplest way for for us
to enforce this was to consistently
apply um Sonar Cube quality stage gates
across all our code bases regardless of
what that code bases for application
codebase or infrastructure as code code
base they're all treated the same way.
So if the gate fails, the stage fails.
The stage fails, the pipeline fails. And
you know, nothing moves on until it's
resolved. And that's the whole point,
right? It gives us the consistent
enforcement without having to rely on,
you know, a senior or a key member in
the team remembering to check a
dashboard to make sure they're okay to
approve a pipeline, for example.
Um, and as I said, infrastructure is
code. We follow the same pattern. you
know, develop a developer or a cloud ops
engineer or a cloud engineer, you know,
they develop a change, push it to a Git
repository, create a pull request for it
to be merged into a base branch. That
would fire multiple automated checks and
one when an automated check runs and the
only way for it to progress is for the
check to pass. Um, and until the check
passes, we do not move on to a human
review. Um,
one of the mandatory checks is you know
the static code analysis which is sonar
cube for example. And again going back
to my original previous example about
minimum code coverage. If the pipeline
um if the pull request runs and minimum
code coverage threshold isn't met out of
the stage gate then the pipeline will
stop. The only way to progress is for
the developer to go back, increase the
code coverage, push the change again,
raise the pull request again, go through
all the um stage gates and checks and
make sure the stage gates pass before
it's handed over to a human for a manual
review. So the main point here um Anuban
is that we're pushing the feedback to as
early as possible. So it makes it a lot
cheaper to fix an issue from a
commercial standpoint and it also avoids
late surprises, delivery surprises and
we treat these checks as mandatory and
it's not something that the teams or you
know regardless of what team you are,
it's not something you can just quietly
turn it off when you're under pressure
and just turn it back on when you know
it's feasible.
That makes complete sense AB and and one
of the running themes that I hear as I
hear you talk about this is that by
virtue of having this regime of uh all
these guard rails deployed across the
entire organization uh you've managed to
reduce risk to the business right
because by protecting every code
pipeline you have you're reducing the
risk of outages the risk of security
incidents and that has real positive
consequences for for for real
Australians right because you're
managing their money you're managing
their health the cost of those outages
is pretty high. So, I wanted to ask you
about that like like do do the the
people on your team feel that sense of
safety that they're able to innovate
faster that they're able to deliver more
value to your customers because of these
guards that are in place.
Um I think that that that demands a
two-faceted answer and so um first facet
I'd say from a from a developer
experience um look no developer likes
stage gets in their in their you know in
their way cuz you know
>> right
>> imagine standing up in a scrum and
trying to explain to your scrum master
the reason why you know your ticket is
delayed by 3 days is because it can't
pass a stage gate for example just
frustrating but on the other hand um The
important part here is um regulatory
compliance along with security, right?
Yes, the devs want speed and velocity.
The business wants velocity, but it
cannot come at the cost of safety for a
couple of reasons. Number one, as you
said, the stakes are high in the
industry and the business that we
operate in. Number two, in Australia,
the compliance requirements are very um
direct and straight in this respect. For
example, um we have recently had our
regulator APA release a new standard
again wouldn't say new but um the
standard vent live CPS 230 which is
purely about how a regulated
organization demonstrate their
capability to be resilient and operate
critical infrastructure and critical
services without risk of outages or
without risk of disruptions. Right? So
us being a regulated organization, our
key idea or a key um thought process or
analogy here is that the speed and
safety are not opposites. What slows the
team down is uncertaintity.
When the team has a different process,
different rules, you know, you waste
time debating results and chasing
exceptions and you know finding a way
around things. But by moving to a
central standard, we've made our rules
consistent for the organization. Sonar
cube is now the enterprise SAS standard.
Practical benefit here is very simple.
There's early detection which leads to
less rework and a more consistent
experience and also exp result across
different teams. So the consistency is
what helps de developers and the dev
delivery teams move faster without
having to feel like they're taking
shortcuts. So they are planning
accordingly. they're estimating their
work accordingly and setting up real
expectations rather than trying to cut
shortcuts and move around um at the cost
of security. The second part I'd say is
the actual trust in the the signal or
the the output that you get. Right? So
I'll give you an example. When we were
moving from the on-prem to the cloud
environment, we spent a lot of time
validating that the key measures were
lining up between our previous and the
new approaches, right? And then we tuned
our build configurations. So things like
code coverage were reported correctly
and consistently.
Once a developer trusts the result, they
stop fighting it and they start using
it. Right? Um
I'll use another way to explain you know
the whole um speed versus velocity sorry
velocity versus security thing. Um I I
use a road analogy right a road safety
analogy. So in in a road safety research
um they they found that even a small
increase in speed has a measurable in
impact. There was a fact sheet that was
published by the government of Western
Australia in March 2024 and it
summarizes that every 1 kilometer per
hour increase in average speed is linked
to about 3% higher risk of an injury in
a crash. Right. That's a 300% increase.
>> Yeah. and then a 4 to 5% higher risk of
a fatal crash. Right? That's 4 to 500%
increase. Right? So you cannot
compromise safety in the name of
velocity. Software is very similar. If
you start cutting corners, you start
pushing risk to a little bit to the
right, you might not feel the the the
you might not feel it or see it
immediately, but over time it will add
up and the probability of something
going bad or something happen something
happening uh going south isn't, you
know, it just goes up. So the central
verification platform is is how we
essentially avoid that trade-off. It
keeps the checks early. It keeps them
consistent. creates visibility so the
devs can move quickly without having to
feel like they're gambling. Right? So to
summarize, I'd say we make the safe path
the easy path and that's what keeps the
velocity up.
>> You've mentioned the the the move to
cloud a couple of times and I imagine a
big part of giving developers the easy
path was this migration to the Sonar
Cube SAS platform. I do want to double
click on that because it's interesting.
Um you you mentioned one of the reasons
that that drove that was uh making sure
that you always had the latest uh rules
and analyzers available so that
developers and the code was was being
scanned to the most current strict
standards possible to make sure that you
could tell a regulator yes we use the
best technology possible to scan the
quality of our code. I'm curious when
you made that transition um was there
data that you had to move uh from your
server instance over to the cloud
instance to make sure you didn't lose
historical information?
Um
look yes and no.
I'll address the first part you know
about moving to the cloud. Look the the
change of moving to the cloud was was I
wouldn't say an easy one but wouldn't
say it was hard either. It was mostly
about getting the prep work right. Um
and we did that by making it as boring
as we possibly could. And and I mean
that in a good way because operating in
a regulated environment requires a lot
of adherence to a lot of governance and
controls. So our aim was to make sure
that the teams had a consistence exper
consistent experience on the day one
right there was no there was no going
back. Um and then we wanted to make sure
that they didn't lose the trust in the
platform on the first.
So we treated this whole migration as a
brand new platform roll out um including
a full operationalization of the
platform before we even did a mass roll
out or migration. So that meant that you
know we were doing a lot of writing down
of the basics like how do users get
onboarded how does user request access
what does a good look like how do you
make changes to your project ourback
controls SSO integration and when you
don't know what you're doing what is
what does a support path look like we
use you know ITSM tool internally so we
had to create new cataloges and forms in
there for or update the existing ones to
allow the developers to in to reach out
to my teams to support them, you know,
when when uh when they're when they're
at a point where they need help. So,
that was the boring part, right? But
it's what lets you scale. It what's it
is what enables you to um essentially
roll out a tool like this across
different teams operating in different
industries looking you know regulated by
multiple div regulators but at the same
time different level of skills within
the teams right and that do without
creating a chaos
and once we did this once the platform
was ready we essentially made sure we
picked up some um pilot workloads if you
may and then we validated the transition
using that pilot workload. So we
executed scans across legacy and the
cloud environment simultaneously on the
same set of code in the one pipeline and
then compared the results and we made
sure that we understood what was
consistent and what would change and
most of the times the newer rules that
we were now applying were essentially
leading to increased findings which were
treated um by moving which were created
because we moved to a new platform
Right.
>> Um and we essentially managed that as a
signal and not a failure. We worked with
the u and look I have to commend our
account team Ben um from from you know
our account team here and then the sonar
cube support team because we were
raising requests left right and center
asking them questions around why is this
different? Why is the code coverage
different? And a lot of that came down
to configuring our scan scan results
properly configuring the scanner
properly. Um and and hence that's what
led to us
earning the developers trust in the
platform. Um which essentially led to a
I between you and me I've been working
in the IT industry for you know over a
decade and I've looked after multiple
cloud transformations migrations DevOps
tooling transformations. I would have to
say this was one of the smoothest
migrations at all because we had no push
back from devs. there was no failures
because we did everything right from the
day one. Um
that that was I guess the key here was
making sure we earn the developers trust
and not just assume the trust. Now um
the second part of the question about
historical data and that's the important
bit right we know that there is no
direct path to migrate the historical
data from the server to the cloud right
um however as a regulated organization
we have internal and external compliance
we've got an internal audit and external
audit compliance requirement that
mandates us to manage historical data
right for a particular set of time frame
um and we we address that through
backups and archival so we backed up. We
archived all the legacy data. Um but on
the other hand to improve our developer
and delivery um experience, you know,
imagine having to going back to a team
to ask for, you know, data from a from
an archive can can be a painful process.
So what we did was we kept our legacy
environment in readonly mode alive for a
while. Um, and what that did was it
provided the dev teams the ability to
look at the historical data in the
legacy system if they needed to until
the cloud environments had now built
some history of its own, right? So
they've got something to compare to. So
the idea here is that there will be a
natural progression of the develop
delivery teams moving away from having
to reuse or look at or address the old
data, compare it with the new one to
find the findings, right? And then once
we get to a point we will then
decommission the on-prem environment um
and and you know take that away from
readily available to just a archive of
data that is kept for uh internal and
external compliance um requirements. I
believe that this approach um keeps us
honest compliant while reducing risk at
the same time.
>> Yeah. No, it totally makes sense and I
think it's a pretty common approach that
our customers take if they're taking the
exact same path as you did by by moving
by trying to keep some of their data
accessible uh on their server instance
as they try to get their team spun up on
cloud. I'm curious now that you're
pretty much entirely on center cube
cloud now has the scale at which you're
using the platform uh increased at all
are you seeing are you processing more
lines of code through there like what's
driving that? Look absolutely 100% um we
have significantly increased the line of
code that are under analysis in the last
quarter and um with the way we are
currently onboarding um different
projects into Sonar Cube including a
mass roll out across um all our tech
stack that is supported. We're looking
at significantly we we're looking at
continuing to significantly increase the
line of code that is managed by the tool
for at least another two to three
quarters. Um
the other aspect of this um increase I'd
say is you know the consumption and the
evolution of AI. Um I as an example I
have a couple of dev developers in one
of the projects that I'm actively um
that that I'm actively supporting um in
our environment. These two devs are
really um they're really heavy users of
AI um coder systems right and as you as
you may know a lot of these code assist
technologies they've got a quarter on a
amount of you know volume of um volume
of um volume of data that the teams can
process or codes that that a developer
can generate. These two devs, they ran
out of their quarter three days in,
right? So the volume of code that they
were generating was huge. So I I really
like where this question is going though
to be very honest cuz this this is a
real change and it's in front of all of
us, right? AI is genuinely increasing
the volume of code, the frequency of
change that any organization, any
development team or delivery teams are
currently generating on a daily basis
significantly higher than before. So
that is really good for productivity,
right? But it also means that our old
approaches of relying on senior devs or
senior you know engineers in the team to
manually spot things and manually spot
anomalies just doesn't scale anymore. It
doesn't fit the purpose anymore. So as
as a cloud first foundation,
it's helped us like this is a problem
that we're facing daily as well, right?
It's helped us in three practical ways
to be honest. The first one is the
verification is now a service and it is
essentially to scale
>> right
>> we've moved the model that was designed
to be used broadly and we've essentially
expanded it across to all our teams so
it's not just a subset of team that's
using it. So the verifications are now
consistent but also easy to consume
because they're automatically integrated
to your pipelines and your pull requests
and it stops becoming a bottleneck
because now we're not restricted to just
you know an on-prem self-manage
instance. Um second is you know as you
as I've said before and as you've
alluded to as well the currency of the
signals that we're getting in a
regulated environment the risk of
self-managed tool is that you can fall
behind on upgrades and rules and when
that happens you have a false sense of
confidence. A managed platform
essentially reduces that dependence on
manual maintenance right and also
reduces operational expense or opex cost
as well and also keeps your security
analysis always current. And third one
and the finally that it supports um
supports our risk where it's moving. So
more of our risk now sits in our
automation code and configuration and
not just the application code as I've
alluded before, right? There's so many
other pathways. The attack surface has
grown significantly in the last few
years. So we've explicitly extended all
the assessments to all our ISC code as
well regardless of which cloud platform
you're writing the IC for, right? And so
we're verifying a lot more than just a
traditional application code as we used
to. And just to touch on AI, right? We
as an organization I believe are quite
mature when it comes to managing AI. We
our take on AI is is essentially a
governance first approach. We have an
internal AI governance process that has
to be completed before we onboard any
new AI tools or switch on any AI related
features in any of the tools. Um, and it
includes how we assess the risk, who
signs off on the risk and how do we keep
an audit trail of of you know what's
happened. And finally, um, just to sum
this up, I'd say the goal for 2026 for
us is simple. um as the volume of the
code grows we have shifted left we have
enforced consistent and automated
verifications for all our code bases. So
moving and moving security close to
where our developer works helps in
keeping velocity without letting the
risk creep in.
>> Yeah. No, I couldn't agree more and and
sentiments like that I've also heard
from a ton of other customers I've been
talking to as part of uh this summit. I
think we could talk for another hour or
two on this topic, but I feel like
you've been very generous with your time
already. Ab thank you very much uh for
having this conversation and thank you
for joining us in summit. I know our
audience really appreciates it and for
our audience, thank you for listening
and hopefully uh you'll stick around for
some of the other great content we have
kit up for you. Thank you.

fullText: Hello folks and welcome to a conversation with Australian Unity. I'm very excited to be joined uh by A Char Sharma who heads up cloud DevOps and mobile practices at Australian Unity. A welcome. >> Thanks, Senator. Appreciate >> it. I'm excited to have a conversation with you. Would you give us a little bit of an intro uh to start us off with just what Australia Unity is and then what your team does? >> Yeah, absolutely. Thanks and um again um hi folks, my name is Aay and I head up the public cloud, mobile and DevOps practices at Australian Unity. Um, Australian Unity is an Australian organization uh with a very simple but powerful purpose which is to um positively impact well-being of millions of Australians. Our vision is to enable what we call real well-being for our members, customers, and our broader community through a portfolio of commercial and sustainable businesses.
